design_philosophy.core_loop: Destroy descending alien formations while avoiding projectiles and collisions; capture and rescue ships to gain dual-fighter power-ups
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward of allowing Boss Galaga to capture your ship for the dual-fighter bonus versus losing a life and control
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Pilot a lone starfighter defending humanity against overwhelming alien forces; accumulate power through rescued companions
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Galaga innovates on Galaxian by introducing the tractor beam capture mechanic, creating a strategic decision tree: risk capturing to gain firepower or play safe. The dual-fighter becomes a significant advantage but introduces a larger hitbox, balancing power with vulnerability. The game is deeply committed to arcade-style risk management rather than difficulty scaling alone.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives system; losing a life when hit by projectile, collision, or capture results in ship loss
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpointing; stages progress sequentially with no mid-stage saves. Every third stage is a bonus stage (no enemy fire) providing brief relief
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through the tractor beam decision: player chooses to engage Boss Galaga for capture or avoid it. Difficulty escalates through enemy aggression, projectile density, and dive attack speed as stages progress

progression.upgrade_system: Dual-fighter acquired by rescuing a captured ship from Boss Galaga; provides additional firepower and increased hitbox. Single ships that turn traitor can be rescued in later levels
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stages unlock sequentially; bonus stages occur every third stage with predetermined safe formations
replayability_hooks: Arcade-style scoring incentivizes perfect runs and high-score chasing
Multiple decision branches based on tractor beam engagement strategy
Enemy morphing variations (Galaxian Flagship) add pattern unpredictability
Kill screen at stage 255 creates a theoretical endgame target for dedicated players

world_building_techniques: Stage emblems indicate progression and world advancement
Enemy formation designs and boss variants suggest alien hierarchy and strategy
Tractor beam mechanic implies alien technology and threat level

boss_design.philosophy: Boss Galaga units function as mini-bosses within each stage formation rather than singular boss encounters. They serve as difficulty gates and decision points. The capture mechanic transforms them from pure combat threats into strategic opportunities. Bosses are not pattern-heavy but rather anchors for the dual-fighter upgrade system.
notable_bosses: Boss Galaga - Large aliens with tractor beam capture ability; require two shots to destroy; appear at top of formation and dive to capture player ships; central to stage strategy

faction_indicators: Alien Galaga forces vs. human starfighter (clear adversarial divide)
Captured ships that turn traitor and rejoin enemy formation (betrayal/coercion mechanic)
Dual-fighter concept implies companionship and mutual survival
danger_signals: Tractor beam as forced capture weapon signals technological dominance threat
Increasing aggression and projectile density per stage indicates escalating alien offensive
Boss Galaga formations at top of screen represent command structure and organized threat
environmental_storytelling: Formation behavior and morphing patterns suggest coordinated alien military strategy
Bonus stages (no fire) provide narrative breathing room, implying occasional alien hesitation or human counteroffensives
Stage emblems and level progression imply geographical or strategic advance through alien territory

sources_used: wikipedia - Galaga gameplay section, development history, mechanics documentation
